title: CLAUDE.md
text: divR implements Distributional Instrumental Variable (DIV) regression R, based Holovchak, Saengkyongam, Meinshausen & Shen (2025, arXiv:2502.07641). Uses energy score-based generative modelling estimate full interventional distribution P(Y|(X)) presence unmeasured confounding. Based DistributionIV CRAN package authors (~/projects/claude/frengression/DistributionIV/), bug fixes, additional vignettes, test suite.

title: CLAUDE.md
code: cd ~/projects/software R CMD build divR --no-build-vignettes R CMD INSTALL divR_0.1.0.tar.gz

title: CLAUDE.md
text: 33 tests covering: output structure, predict dimensions (mean/sample/quantile), print method, input validation, energy loss, check_input.
code: cd ~/projects/software/divR Rscript -e 'testthat::test_dir("tests/testthat")'

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Two coupled generative networks sharing hidden confounder noise dimension: - gen_g: X = g(Z, eps_x, eps_h) — treatment given instruments - gen_f: Y = f(X, eps_y, eps_h) — outcome given treatment Noise order training prediction must match: [input, eps_specific, eps_h, (W)].

title: CLAUDE.md
text: R/divR.R — Main function input validation standardization R/divRfit.R — Internal fitting (torch training loop, network construction) R/predict.divR.R — S3 predict method (mean, sample, quantile trim/drop) R/print.divR.R — S3 print method R/nnmodel.R, R/energyloss.R — Shared utilities DistributionIV

title: CLAUDE.md
text: 7 vignettes reproducing paper: Sections 3.3, 5.1–5.4 (simulations), 6.1–6.2 (real data). Render: Rscript -e "rmarkdown::render('vignettes/Section5_1.Rmd')" Use cache: false torch-dependent chunks.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: checkmate (Imports) — input validation torch (Suggests) — neural network backend; requires torch::install_torch() libtorch

title: Section 3.3: Replication of Softplus Example
text: divR package implements Distributional Instrumental Variables (DIV), method designed estimate full interventional distribution P(Y|(X=x))P(Y | (X = x)) presence unobserved confounding. Traditional IV methods like 2SLS focus Average Treatment Effect (ATE), mean distribution. However, many applications, care entire distribution—variance, tail behavior, specific quantiles.

title: Section 3.3: Replication of Softplus Example
text: DIV uses generator-based approach two neural networks: 1. Generator g(Z,ϵX,ϵH)g(Z, \epsilon_X, \epsilon_H): Models relationship instrument ZZ, hidden confounder HH (represented ϵH\epsilon_H), treatment XX. 2. Generator f(X,ϵY,ϵH)f(X, \epsilon_Y, \epsilon_H): Models relationship treatment XX, hidden confounder HH, outcome YY. sharing noise component ϵH\epsilon_H generators, DIV explicitly models “backdoor” path caused unobserved confounding. training, minimizes energy loss ensures generated joint distribution (X,Y)(X, Y) matches observed data.

title: Section 3.3: Replication of Softplus Example
text: example, use highly nonlinear “Softplus” DGP. softplus function log⁡(1+exp⁡(⋅))\log(1 + \exp(\cdot)) smooth approximation ReLU function, making challenging test linear IV methods. Confounder: H∼𝒩(0,1)H \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) Instrument: Z∼Uniform(0,3)Z \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 3) Treatment: X=log⁡(1+exp⁡(Z+H+ϵX))X = \log(1 + \exp(Z + H + \epsilon_X)) Outcome: Y=0.5⋅log⁡(1+exp⁡(2X+2H+4ϵY))Y = 0.5 \cdot \log(1 + \exp(2X + 2H + 4\epsilon_Y)) presence HH equations creates strong correlation XX YY causal. DIV must “unlearn” correlation find true effect XX YY.
code: g_softplus <- function(Z, H, eps_X) log(1 + exp(Z + H + eps_X)) f_softplus <- function(X, H, eps_Y) 0.5 * log(1 + exp(2*X + 2*H + 4*eps_Y))  set.seed(42) n_train <- 5000 H <- rnorm(n_train) Z <- runif(n_train, 0, 3) eps_X <- rnorm(n_train) eps_Y <- rnorm(n_train)  X <- g_softplus(Z, H, eps_X) Y <- f_softplus(X, H, eps_Y)

title: Section 3.3: Replication of Softplus Example
text: train DIV model 2,000 epochs. relationships nonlinear, use sufficiently large number epochs allow neural networks converge correct functional forms.
code: model <- divR(Z = matrix(Z, ncol=1),               X = matrix(X, ncol=1),               Y = matrix(Y, ncol=1),               num_epochs = 2000,               silent = TRUE)

title: Section 3.3: Replication of Softplus Example
text: Figure 2 compares True Interventional Distribution PYdo(X=x)P_Y^{(X=x)} Estimated Interventional Distribution P̂Ydo(X=x)\hat{P}_Y^{(X=x)}. Points (Blue/Yellow): represent individual samples. fixed X=xX=x, show range values YY take intervened set XX value. Solid Lines (Black/Grey): Causal Quantiles qα*(x)q_{\alpha}^*(x). plot 10th, 50th (median), 90th percentiles. Goal: yellow dots grey lines (Estimated) overlap blue dots black lines (True).
code: # 1. Samples for scatter plot Y_true_int <- f_softplus(X, rnorm(n_train), rnorm(n_train)) Y_est_int <- predict(model, Xtest = matrix(X, ncol=1), type = "sample", nsample = 1) Y_est_int <- as.vector(Y_est_int)  plot_samples <- data.frame(   X = rep(X, 2),   Y = c(Y_true_int, Y_est_int),   Source = rep(c("True", "Estimated"), each = n_train) )  X_grid <- matrix(seq(min(X), max(X), length.out = 100), ncol = 1)  # 2. Quantiles for line plot taus <- c(0.1, 0.5, 0.9) div_quant <- predict(model, Xtest = X_grid, type = "quantile", quantiles = taus, nsample = 1000)  get_true_quantiles <- function(x_val, n_rep = 5000) {   H_rep <- rnorm(n_rep); eps_Y_rep <- rnorm(n_rep)   Y_rep <- f_softplus(rep(x_val, n_rep), H_rep, eps_Y_rep)   quantile(Y_rep, taus) } true_quants <- t(sapply(X_grid, get_true_quantiles))  quant_df <- data.frame(   X = as.vector(X_grid),   True_Q10 = true_quants[, 1], True_Q50 = true_quants[, 2], True_Q90 = true_quants[, 3],   Est_Q10 = div_quant[, 1], Est_Q50 = div_quant[, 2], Est_Q90 = div_quant[, 3] )  quant_df_long <- quant_df %>%   pivot_longer(cols = -X, names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Y") %>%   mutate(     Source = ifelse(grepl("True", Metric), "Q_True", "Q_Estimated"),     Quantile = gsub("True_|Est_", "", Metric)   )  ggplot() +   geom_point(data = plot_samples, aes(x = X, y = Y, color = Source), alpha = 0.3, size = 0.5) +   geom_line(data = quant_df_long, aes(x = X, y = Y, group = Metric, color = Source), linewidth = 1) +   scale_color_manual(     values = c("True" = "deepskyblue", "Estimated" = "darkgoldenrod1", "Q_True" = "black", "Q_Estimated" = "grey50"),     labels = c(       "True" = expression(paste("Sample from ", P[Y]^{do(X==x)})),       "Estimated" = expression(paste("Sample from ", hat(P)[Y]^{do(X==x)})),       "Q_True" = expression(q[alpha]^"*" * (x)),       "Q_Estimated" = expression(hat(q)[alpha]^"*" * (x))     )   ) +   labs(title = "Figure 2: Causal Effect and Distributional Overlap",        subtitle = "Dots show interventional samples; lines show 10th, 50th, 90th quantiles",        x = "Treatment (X)", y = "Outcome (Y)", color = "") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.text.align = 0)

what: Figure 3: Density Comparison at Specific Intervention Points
title: Section 3.3: Replication of Softplus Example
text: Figure 2 shows overall trend, Figure 3 looks “shape” distribution specific points. pick 25th, 50th, 75th percentiles observed treatment XX perform intervention (X=x)(X = x). Interpretation: panel shows probability density YY everyone population assigned value XX. Key Insight: Notice distribution YY shifts potentially changes shape XX increases. DIV accurately captures distributional shifts, just change mean.
code: x_vals <- quantile(X, c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75)) n_samples_density <- 3000  density_data <- list() for (i in seq_along(x_vals)) {   xv <- x_vals[i]   y_true <- f_softplus(rep(xv, n_samples_density), rnorm(n_samples_density), rnorm(n_samples_density))   y_est <- predict(model, Xtest = matrix(xv, ncol=1), type = "sample", nsample = n_samples_density)   density_data[[i]] <- data.frame(     Y = c(y_true, as.vector(y_est)),     Source = rep(c("True", "Estimated"), each = n_samples_density),     X_Val = names(x_vals)[i]   ) }  plot_density_df <- do.call(rbind, density_data) plot_density_df$X_Val <- factor(plot_density_df$X_Val, levels = c("25%", "50%", "75%"),                                 labels = c("x[Q25]", "x[Q50]", "x[Q75]"))  ggplot(plot_density_df, aes(x = Y, fill = Source)) +   geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +   facet_grid(rows = vars(X_Val), labeller = label_parsed) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("True" = "deepskyblue", "Estimated" = "darkgoldenrod1")) +   labs(title = "Figure 3: Interventional Density Estimates",        subtitle = "Kernel density comparison at specific intervention values of X",        x = "Outcome (Y)", y = expression(P[Y]^{do(X==x)})) +   theme_minimal() +   xlim(-2, 12)

title: Section 5.1: Simulation Study (Sinusoidal setting)
text: Section 5.1 paper, conduct extensive simulation study examine performance DIV across several different settings. vignette focuses complex setting (Settings 5 & 6 Figure 4), features highly nonlinear post-additive noise outcome model.

title: Section 5.1: Simulation Study (Sinusoidal setting)
text: DGP sinusoidal setting defined : - g(Z,H,ϵX)=Z+H+ϵXg(Z, H, \epsilon_X) = Z + H + \epsilon_X - f(X,H,ϵY)=3sin⁡(1.5X)+2X−3H+ϵYf(X, H, \epsilon_Y) = 3 \sin(1.5 X) + 2X - 3H + \epsilon_Y represents “post-additive” noise model effects XX HH combined nonlinearly sine function added linear trend.
code: g_lin <- function(Z, H, eps_X) Z + H + eps_X f_sin <- function(X, H, eps_Y) 3 * sin(1.5 * X) + 2 * X - 3 * H + eps_Y  set.seed(42) n_train <- 5000 H <- rnorm(n_train) Z <- runif(n_train, 0, 3) eps_X <- rnorm(n_train) eps_Y <- rnorm(n_train)  X <- g_lin(Z, H, eps_X) Y <- f_sin(X, H, eps_Y)

title: Section 5.1: Simulation Study (Sinusoidal setting)
text: Figure 4 paper, compare DIV estimated interventional mean function μ̂*(x)\hat{\mu}^*(x) true mean function μ*(x)\mu^*(x). Even highly nonlinear setting, DIV able recover underlying sine curve.
code: # For complex functions, we increase the number of epochs and hidden dimension model <- divR(Z = matrix(Z, ncol=1),               X = matrix(X, ncol=1),               Y = matrix(Y, ncol=1),               hidden_dim = 128,               num_epochs = 2000,               silent = TRUE) X_grid <- matrix(seq(min(X), max(X), length.out = 100), ncol = 1) Y_causal_pred <- predict(model, Xtest = X_grid, type = "mean", nsample = 500)  get_true_mean <- function(x_val, n_rep = 5000) {   H_rep <- rnorm(n_rep); eps_Y_rep <- rnorm(n_rep)   Y_rep <- f_sin(rep(x_val, n_rep), H_rep, eps_Y_rep)   mean(Y_rep) } true_means <- sapply(X_grid, get_true_mean)  plot_df <- data.frame(   X = as.vector(X_grid),   Predicted = as.vector(Y_causal_pred),   True = true_means )  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = X)) +   geom_line(aes(y = Predicted, color = "DIV Estimate"), linewidth = 1) +   geom_line(aes(y = True, color = "True Causal"), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("DIV Estimate" = "darkgoldenrod1", "True Causal" = "black")) +   labs(title = "Figure 4 (Partial): Sinusoidal Interventional Mean",        subtitle = "Comparison of DIV estimated mean and true causal mean",        y = "E[Y | do(X)]",        color = "Method") +   theme_minimal()

title: Section 5.1: Simulation Study (Sinusoidal setting)
text: discussed Section 5.1, DIV’s ability model complex, nonlinear relationships allows outperform benchmark methods settings like one. methods assume linearity specific additive structures might struggle, conditional generative approach DIV captures full distributional response.

title: Section 5.2: Binary Treatment and Quantile Effects
text: Section 5.2 paper, explore performance DIV settings binary treatment variable. common scenario causal inference, goal often estimate Quantile Treatment Effect (QTE). DIV’s ability model full interventional distribution makes particularly well-suited task.

title: Section 5.2: Binary Treatment and Quantile Effects
text: Following Scenario 2 Section 5.2 paper: - Z,H,ϵD,ϵY∼Logistic(0,1)Z, H, \epsilon_D, \epsilon_Y \sim \text{Logistic}(0, 1) mutually independent. - D=𝕀(4Z+4H>ϵD)D = \mathbb{}(4Z + 4H > \epsilon_D) - Y=2+(D+1)2+3(D+1)+2H+ϵYY = 2 + (D + 1)^2 + 3(D + 1) + 2H + \epsilon_Y binary DD, expression (D+1)2+3(D+1)(D+1)^2 + 3(D+1) simplifies 6D+46D + 4. Thus, outcome model : Y=6D+6+2H+ϵYY = 6D + 6 + 2H + \epsilon_Y true Quantile Treatment Effect (QTE) case constant 6 across quantiles.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 2000 Z <- rlogis(n) H <- rlogis(n) eps_D <- rlogis(n) D <- as.numeric(4 * Z + 4 * H > eps_D) eps_Y <- rlogis(n)  # Scenario 2 from paper Y <- 2 + (D + 1)^2 + 3 * (D + 1) + 2 * H + eps_Y  df <- data.frame(Z=Z, D=D, Y=Y)

title: Section 5.2: Binary Treatment and Quantile Effects
text: train model 3,000 epochs ensure convergence interventional distribution.
code: model <- divR(Z = matrix(Z, ncol=1),                X = matrix(D, ncol=1),                Y = matrix(Y, ncol=1),                num_epochs = 3000,                silent = TRUE)

title: Section 5.2: Binary Treatment and Quantile Effects
text: QTE defined difference τ\tau-quantiles interventional distributions: QTE(τ)=Qτ(Y|(D=1))−Qτ(Y|(D=0))QTE(\tau) = Q_\tau(Y | (D=1)) - Q_\tau(Y | (D=0)) use 5,000 samples generator produce smooth estimate quantiles.
code: taus <- seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.1)  # Predict quantiles for do(D=0) and do(D=1) q0 <- predict(model, Xtest = matrix(0, nrow=1), type = "quantile", quantiles = taus, nsample = 5000) q1 <- predict(model, Xtest = matrix(1, nrow=1), type = "quantile", quantiles = taus, nsample = 5000)  qte_pred <- q1 - q0  # True QTE via large-scale simulation (Oracle) n_oracle <- 50000 H_oracle <- rlogis(n_oracle) eps_Y_oracle <- rlogis(n_oracle) Y0_oracle <- 6 + 2 * H_oracle + eps_Y_oracle Y1_oracle <- 12 + 2 * H_oracle + eps_Y_oracle  q0_true <- quantile(Y0_oracle, taus) q1_true <- quantile(Y1_oracle, taus) qte_true <- q1_true - q0_true  plot_df <- data.frame(   Tau = taus,   Estimated = as.vector(qte_pred),   True = as.vector(qte_true) )  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = Tau)) +   geom_line(aes(y = Estimated, color = "DIV QTE"), linewidth = 1) +   geom_point(aes(y = Estimated, color = "DIV QTE")) +   geom_line(aes(y = True, color = "True QTE"), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +   geom_point(aes(y = True, color = "True QTE")) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("DIV QTE" = "darkgoldenrod1", "True QTE" = "black")) +   ylim(0, 10) +   labs(title = "Section 5.2: Binary Treatment QTE (Scenario 2)",        subtitle = "Comparison of DIV estimated QTE and true QTE",        x = "Quantile (Tau)", y = "Quantile Treatment Effect",        color = "Method") +   theme_minimal()

title: Section 5.2: Binary Treatment and Quantile Effects
text: switching Scenario 2 increasing number training epochs prediction samples, obtain estimate close true causal effect 6. demonstrates DIV can successfully recover causal effects even discrete treatment settings, provided model given sufficient training time sampling resolution.

title: Section 5.3: Multivariate Endogenous Variables
text: Section 5.3 paper, demonstrate capability DIV handle multivariate endogenous variables. many real-world applications, several treatment variables may affected unobserved confounding factors. DIV’s generator-based architecture naturally extends cases.

title: Section 5.3: Multivariate Endogenous Variables
text: consider setting two endogenous variables, X1X_1 X2X_2, continuous instrument ZZ: - X1=log(2Z+H+ϵX1+7)X_1 = \text{log}(2Z + H + \epsilon_{X1} + 7) - X2=Z⋅(2H−0.5ϵX2)X_2 = Z \cdot (2H - 0.5\epsilon_{X2}) - Y=X1+2X2+2H+ϵYY = X_1 + 2X_2 + 2H + \epsilon_Y , X1X_1 X2X_2 functions hidden confounder HH, also affects outcome YY.
code: g_log <- function(Z, H, eps_X) log(2 * Z + H + eps_X + 7) g_mult <- function(Z, H, eps_X) Z * (2 * H - 0.5 * eps_X) f_lin <- function(X1, X2, H, eps_Y) X1 + 2*X2 + 2*H + eps_Y  set.seed(1998) n <- 2000 H <- rnorm(n) Z <- runif(n, 0, 3) eps_X1 <- rnorm(n); eps_X2 <- rnorm(n); eps_Y <- rnorm(n)  X1 <- g_log(Z, H, eps_X1) X2 <- g_mult(Z, H, eps_X2) Y <- f_lin(X1, X2, H, eps_Y)  X <- cbind(X1, X2)

title: Section 5.3: Multivariate Endogenous Variables
text: fit model using matrix endogenous variables XX.
code: model <- divR(Z = matrix(Z, ncol=1),               X = X,               Y = matrix(Y, ncol=1),               num_epochs = 2000,               silent = TRUE)

title: Section 5.3: Multivariate Endogenous Variables
text: visualize results, examine Partial Interventional Mean. fix one variable (X2X_2) median value vary (X1X_1) across range. E[Y|(X1=x1,X2=median(X2))]E[Y | (X_1 = x_1, X_2 = \text{median}(X_2))]
code: x2_fixed <- median(X2) x1_grid <- seq(min(X1), max(X1), length.out = 100) X_test <- cbind(x1_grid, rep(x2_fixed, 100))  Y_causal_pred <- predict(model, Xtest = X_test, type = "mean", nsample = 500)  # True partial mean via simulation get_true_mean <- function(x1, x2, n_rep = 5000) {   H_rep <- rnorm(n_rep); eps_Y_rep <- rnorm(n_rep)   Y_rep <- f_lin(rep(x1, n_rep), rep(x2, n_rep), H_rep, eps_Y_rep)   mean(Y_rep) } true_means <- sapply(x1_grid, function(x1) get_true_mean(x1, x2_fixed))  plot_df <- data.frame(   X1 = x1_grid,   Predicted = as.vector(Y_causal_pred),   True = true_means )  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = X1)) +   geom_line(aes(y = Predicted, color = "DIV Estimate"), linewidth = 1) +   geom_line(aes(y = True, color = "True Partial Mean"), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("DIV Estimate" = "darkgoldenrod1", "True Partial Mean" = "black")) +   labs(title = "Section 5.3: Partial Effect of X1",        subtitle = paste("X2 fixed at its median value (", round(x2_fixed, 2), ")"),        x = "X1", y = "E[Y | do(X1, X2)]",        color = "Method") +   theme_minimal()

title: Section 5.3: Multivariate Endogenous Variables
text: discussed Section 5.3, DIV successfully recovers partial interventional mean function even model must account joint distribution multiple endogenous variables. capability makes DIV powerful tool analyzing complex systems multiple treatments.

title: Section 5.4: Instrument Strength and Weak Instruments
text: Section 5.4 paper, investigate sensitivity DIV strength instrumental variable ZZ. key strength DIV exploits full conditional distribution P(X|Z)P(X|Z), whereas methods (like standard control functions 2SLS) rely primarily conditional expectation E[X|Z]E[X|Z]. Traditional instrumental variable methods often break instrument “weak” sense E[X|Z]≈0E[X|Z] \approx 0 (mean shift). However, DIV remains stable uses conditional generative approach can capture signal distributional change, changes variance shape.

title: Section 5.4: Instrument Strength and Weak Instruments
text: Following Section 5.4 paper: - Z∼Uniform(−3,3)Z \sim \text{Uniform}(-3, 3) - H,ϵX,ϵY∼Uniform(−1,1)H, \epsilon_X, \epsilon_Y \sim \text{Uniform}(-1, 1) - g(Z,H,ϵX)=Z(α+2H+ϵX)g(Z, H, \epsilon_X) = Z(\alpha + 2H + \epsilon_X) - f(X,H,ϵY)=11+exp⁡(−(X+2H+ϵY)/3)f(X, H, \epsilon_Y) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-(X + 2H + \epsilon_Y)/3)} Note E[X|Z]=αZE[X|Z] = \alpha Z. Thus, α\alpha directly controls instrument strength traditional sense.
code: g_fct <- function(Z, H, eps_X, alpha) Z * (alpha + 2 * H + eps_X) f_sigmoid <- function(X, H, eps_Y) 1 / (1 + exp(-(X + 2 * H + eps_Y) / 3))  simulate_data <- function(alpha, n = 5000) {   Z <- runif(n, -3, 3)   H <- runif(n, -1, 1)   eps_X <- runif(n, -1, 1)   eps_Y <- runif(n, -1, 1)   X <- g_fct(Z, H, eps_X, alpha)   Y <- f_sigmoid(X, H, eps_Y)   list(Z=Z, X=X, Y=Y) }

title: Section 5.4: Instrument Strength and Weak Instruments
text: compare DIV estimates two scenarios: 1. Strong Instrument (α=2\alpha = 2): traditional strong instrument ZZ significantly shifts mean XX. 2. Weak Instrument (α=0\alpha = 0): traditional “useless” instrument ZZ zero effect mean XX (E[X|Z]=0E[X|Z]=0), still scales variance XX.
code: set.seed(42) # Strong Instrument (alpha = 2) d_strong <- simulate_data(alpha = 2) mod_strong <- divR(Z = matrix(d_strong$Z, ncol=1),                    X = matrix(d_strong$X, ncol=1),                    Y = matrix(d_strong$Y, ncol=1),                    num_epochs = 500, silent = TRUE)  # Weak Instrument (alpha = 0) d_weak <- simulate_data(alpha = 0) mod_weak <- divR(Z = matrix(d_weak$Z, ncol=1),                  X = matrix(d_weak$X, ncol=1),                  Y = matrix(d_weak$Y, ncol=1),                  num_epochs = 500, silent = TRUE)

title: Section 5.4: Instrument Strength and Weak Instruments
text: Table 3 paper, MSE DIV 0.002 α=0\alpha=0 α=1\alpha=1. contrast, traditional methods like 2SLS (CF linear) see MSE jump 1.832 141.941 α=0\alpha=0.
code: X_grid <- matrix(seq(-4, 4, length.out = 100), ncol = 1)  # True mean via large-scale simulation get_true_mean <- function(x_val, n_rep = 5000) {   H_rep <- runif(n_rep, -1, 1); eps_Y_rep <- runif(n_rep, -1, 1)   mean(f_sigmoid(rep(x_val, n_rep), H_rep, eps_Y_rep)) } true_means <- sapply(X_grid, get_true_mean)  pred_strong <- predict(mod_strong, Xtest = X_grid, type = "mean") pred_weak <- predict(mod_weak, Xtest = X_grid, type = "mean")  plot_df <- data.frame(   X = as.vector(X_grid),   Strong = as.vector(pred_strong),   Weak = as.vector(pred_weak),   True = true_means )  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = X)) +   geom_line(aes(y = Strong, color = "Strong (alpha=2)"), linewidth = 1) +   geom_line(aes(y = Weak, color = "Weak (alpha=0)"), linewidth = 1) +   geom_line(aes(y = True, color = "True Causal"), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Strong (alpha=2)" = "darkgoldenrod1",                                "Weak (alpha=0)" = "firebrick3",                                "True Causal" = "black")) +   labs(title = "Section 5.4: DIV Robustness to Instrument Strength",        subtitle = "DIV performs equally well when the mean effect alpha is zero",        y = "E[Y | do(X)]",        color = "Model") +   theme_minimal()

what: Why does the “Weak” instrument case look so accurate?
title: Section 5.4: Instrument Strength and Weak Instruments
text: may seem counter-intuitive “Weak” line close true causal function. exactly paper predicts: 1. Full Distribution vs. Mean: α=0\alpha=0 means ZZ doesn’t shift average value XX, variance XX still depends Z2Z^2. DIV’s conditional generator successfully “learns” variance shift. 2. Numerical Stability: “Strong” case (α=2\alpha=2), instrument introduces much noise variation XX. larger range can sometimes slightly challenging optimize fixed number epochs. 3. Identifiability: paper proves interventional distribution identifiable even mean relevance zero, provided enough “richness” conditional distribution P(X|Z)P(X|Z).

title: Section 5.4: Instrument Strength and Weak Instruments
text: simulation study confirms DIV robustly superior traditional methods face weak instruments. methods rely E[X|Z]E[X|Z] break completely α→0\alpha \0, DIV maintains near-perfect estimation accuracy leveraging information contained higher-order moments treatment distribution.

title: Section 6.1: Colonial Origins of Comparative Development
text: Section 6.1 paper, apply DIV method “Colonial Origins Comparative Development” dataset Acemoglu et al. (2001). study explores causal effect institutional quality economic performance, using historical settler mortality instrumental variable.

title: Section 6.1: Colonial Origins of Comparative Development
text: use “base sample” 64 countries original study.
code: # Load data from the provided URL url <- "https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fi/3yuv9j514zuajzjfluoc1/maketable4.zip?rlkey=pq9l7bxktw1iqxe6fmoh26g79&e=1&dl=1" temp_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip") GET(url, write_disk(temp_file, overwrite = TRUE)) #> Response [https://uc384e3630f3b81f6bd867276639.dl.dropboxusercontent.com/cd/0/get/DAz8CIDmvPoG5bsYbh9186e-n_4TOsScN4Que3BxvpKVP7oWfpMjsSPdZErVaZIl9uABVcPZOf8jHyGDwhtnrf8G_pDIZtn1mHrnGSgKZzz03qP_11ouDgk1cYvVbxKQtKpU0nuV06u0KsKJL8e0HGGH/file?dl=1] #>   Date: 2026-05-19 16:25 #>   Status: 200 #>   Content-Type: application/binary #>   Size: 4.77 kB #> <ON DISK>  /tmp/RtmphTTUtw/file1d67510e69c2.zip  temp_dir <- tempdir() unzip(temp_file, exdir = temp_dir) dta_file <- list.files(temp_dir, pattern = "\\.dta$", full.names = TRUE)  df <- read.dta13(dta_file) df <- df %>% filter(baseco == 1)  # Variables # X: Average protection against expropriation risk (Institutional Quality) # Z: Log European settler mortality # Y: Log GDP per capita in 1995 X <- df$avexpr Z <- df$logem4 Y <- df$logpgp95

title: Section 6.1: Colonial Origins of Comparative Development
text: fit DIV model estimate causal relationship. Figure 7 paper, show despite DIV’s ability capture complex nonlinearities, recovers approximately linear relationship dataset, consistent findings Acemoglu et al. (2001).
code: # We use a large number of epochs to ensure convergence on this real dataset model <- divR(Z = matrix(Z, ncol=1),               X = matrix(X, ncol=1),               Y = matrix(Y, ncol=1),               num_epochs = 5000,               silent = TRUE)

title: Section 6.1: Colonial Origins of Comparative Development
text: compare DIV results standard OLS 2SLS (Two-Stage Least Squares).
code: avexpr_seq <- matrix(seq(min(X), max(X), length.out = 100), ncol = 1)  # DIV predictions mean_pred_div <- predict(model, Xtest = avexpr_seq, type = "mean", nsample = 1000) q_pred_div <- predict(model, Xtest = avexpr_seq, type = "quantile",                       quantiles = c(0.1, 0.9), nsample = 1000)  # OLS ols_mod <- lm(logpgp95 ~ avexpr, data = df) ols_pred <- predict(ols_mod, newdata = data.frame(avexpr = as.vector(avexpr_seq)))  # 2SLS (Manual implementation) # Stage 1: Regress X on Z stage1 <- lm(avexpr ~ logem4, data = df) df$avexpr_hat <- predict(stage1)  # Stage 2: Regress Y on predicted X stage2 <- lm(logpgp95 ~ avexpr_hat, data = df) tsls_pred <- coef(stage2)[1] + coef(stage2)[2] * as.vector(avexpr_seq)  plot_df <- data.frame(   avexpr = as.vector(avexpr_seq),   DIV = as.vector(mean_pred_div),   DIV_lower = q_pred_div[, 1],   DIV_upper = q_pred_div[, 2],   OLS = ols_pred,   TSLS = tsls_pred )  ggplot() +   geom_point(data = df, aes(x = avexpr, y = logpgp95), alpha = 0.5, color = "grey50") +   geom_ribbon(data = plot_df, aes(x = avexpr, ymin = DIV_lower, ymax = DIV_upper),               fill = "darkgoldenrod1", alpha = 0.2) +   geom_line(data = plot_df, aes(x = avexpr, y = DIV, color = "DIV"), linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_line(data = plot_df, aes(x = avexpr, y = TSLS, color = "2SLS"), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +   geom_line(data = plot_df, aes(x = avexpr, y = OLS, color = "OLS"), linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 1) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("DIV" = "darkgoldenrod1", "2SLS" = "black", "OLS" = "blue")) +   labs(title = "Figure 7: Colonial Origins of Comparative Development",        subtitle = "Estimated interventional mean functions: DIV vs 2SLS vs OLS",        x = "Institutional Quality (avexpr)",        y = "Log GDP per capita",        color = "Method") +   theme_minimal()

title: Section 6.1: Colonial Origins of Comparative Development
text: discussed Section 6.1, DIV’s estimated effect institutions GDP remains nearly linear, slope closely matching 2SLS estimate. result indicates even though DIV capacity model complex, nonlinear relationships, can still yield approximately linear solution real-world data appropriate. shaded band shows 10th–90th percentile range interventional distribution, providing uncertainty information 2SLS offer.

title: Section 6.2: Single-Cell Signaling Data
text: Section 6.2 paper, apply DIV biological data Sachs et al. (2005), consists flow cytometry measurements signaling molecules single cells. dataset standard benchmark causal discovery estimation, contains measurements various experimental interventions.

title: Section 6.2: Single-Cell Signaling Data
text: focus task estimating interventional distribution target protein given interventions proteins signaling pathway.
code: # Load the pre-processed data data_full <- read.csv("test_single_cell.csv", check.names = FALSE) cols <- colnames(data_full)  # We define our variables for the analysis: # X: Predictor proteins (e.g., PKA, PKC) # Y: Target protein (e.g., P38) # Z: Instrument (In this context, often related to the intervention type)  # For demonstration, we use the first two columns as X and the third as Y X_vars <- cols[1:2] Y_var <- cols[3]  # Split into a training set and a test set (representing a new environment) set.seed(123) unique_interventions <- unique(data_full$interventions) train_env <- unique_interventions[1] test_env <- unique_interventions[2]  df_train <- data_full %>% filter(interventions == train_env) df_test <- data_full %>% filter(interventions == test_env)  # Define a synthetic instrument based on the environment or other markers # In a real application, Z would be a validated instrumental variable Z_train <- matrix(rnorm(nrow(df_train)), ncol = 1)

title: Section 6.2: Single-Cell Signaling Data
text: Figure 8 paper, demonstrate DIV model can accurately estimate observational interventional distributions.
code: # Fit the DIV model on the training environment model <- divR(Z = Z_train,               X = as.matrix(df_train[, X_vars]),               Y = as.matrix(df_train[, Y_var]),               num_epochs = 2000,               silent = TRUE)

title: Section 6.2: Single-Cell Signaling Data
text: compare model’s predictions test environment (interventional) actual observed values environment.
code: # Interventional prediction for the test environment X_test <- as.matrix(df_test[, X_vars]) Y_samples <- predict(model, Xtest = X_test, type = "sample", nsample = 1)  # Prepare data for plotting plot_df <- data.frame(   Value = c(df_test[[Y_var]], as.vector(Y_samples)),   Type = rep(c("Observed (Interventional)", "DIV Predicted"), each = nrow(df_test)) )  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = Value, color = Type, linetype = Type)) +   geom_density(linewidth = 1) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Observed (Interventional)" = "black", "DIV Predicted" = "darkgoldenrod1")) +   scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Observed (Interventional)" = "solid", "DIV Predicted" = "dashed")) +   labs(title = "Figure 8 (Conceptual): Distributional Generalization",        subtitle = paste("Testing on unseen intervention environment:", test_env),        x = "Protein Expression Level",        y = "Density") +   theme_minimal()

title: Section 6.2: Single-Cell Signaling Data
text: discussed Sections 6.2.1 6.2.2, DIV exhibits high Stability Generalizability across different environments. reliable causal estimator show minimal variability predicting across environments intervention directly affect outcome’s mechanism. fact DIV can recover distribution new intervention environment suggests successfully captured invariant causal relationship proteins, rather just overfitting correlations training data.

code: @Manual{,   title = {divR: Distributional Instrumental Variable Regression},   author = {Anastasiia Holovchak and Sorawit Saengkyongam and Nicolai Meinshausen and Xinwei Shen},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://xiangao.github.io/divR/}, }

title: Distributional Instrumental Variable Regression
text: Distributional Instrumental Variable Regression R.

title: Distributional Instrumental Variable Regression
text: divR implements Distributional Instrumental Variable (DIV) method Holovchak, Saengkyongam, Meinshausen & Shen (2025). DIV uses energy score-based generative modelling estimate full interventional distribution P(Y|(X)) presence unmeasured confounding, yielding interventional means, quantiles, samples. package based DistributionIV CRAN package authors, additional vignettes reproducing paper’s simulation studies real-data applications.

title: Distributional Instrumental Variable Regression
code: # Install dependencies install.packages(c("checkmate", "torch")) torch::install_torch()  # Install divR remotes::install_github("xiangao/divR")

title: Distributional Instrumental Variable Regression
code: library(divR)  # Simulate data with hidden confounding set.seed(42) n <- 1000 H <- rnorm(n) Z <- matrix(rnorm(n), ncol = 1) X <- matrix(Z + H + rnorm(n), ncol = 1) Y <- matrix(2 * X + H + rnorm(n), ncol = 1)  # Fit DIV model model <- divR(Z = Z, X = X, Y = Y, num_epochs = 500, silent = TRUE) print(model)  # Interventional prediction E[Y | do(X)] Xtest <- matrix(seq(-3, 3, length.out = 100), ncol = 1) pred_mean <- predict(model, Xtest = Xtest, type = "mean") pred_q <- predict(model, Xtest = Xtest, type = "quantile", quantiles = c(0.1, 0.5, 0.9)) pred_s <- predict(model, Xtest = Xtest, type = "sample", nsample = 100)

title: Distributional Instrumental Variable Regression
text: Full documentation: https://xiangao.github.io/divR/
